Affiliation (institution(s)) (string or N/A)
Response: Kavraki Lab, Rice University (lead development); Dr. Lydia Kavraki (PI), Dr. Mark Moll, Dr. Zachary Kingston, Dr. Ioan Șucan

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Response: Open-source C++ library for sampling-based motion planning algorithms; provides implementations of planners such as PRM, RRT, RRT*, EST, and many others; designed as a framework for motion planning research and integration into robotics software (e.g., MoveIt, OpenRAVE)

Response: C++, Python (Python bindings via Py++ code generation)

Metric: Is there evidence that performance was considered? Performance refers to either speed, storage, or throughput. (yes∗, no)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: OMPL is the de facto standard C++ motion planning library in academic robotics. It does not include a robot model or collision checking (by design); these must be provided by the user or an integrating framework (MoveIt, ROS, OpenRAVE). OMPL ships with an extensive benchmarking framework for comparing planners. Integrated into MoveIt as default motion planning backend. Latest release: OMPL 1.7.0 (April 7, 2025).

Response: Extremely easy installation on Ubuntu via apt. Among the simplest C++ library installs reviewed. The apt package includes both the library and Python bindings. Prebuilt packages available on Ubuntu, macOS Homebrew, and via vcpkg on Windows.

Response: Extremely stable library. Planners are probabilistically complete by design and documented as such. No runtime crashes during tutorial testing.

Response: Extremely reusable by design — OMPL is explicitly designed as a library to be integrated into other frameworks. Used in MoveIt, OpenRAVE, Gazebo, and numerous custom robotics systems. Plugin architecture for custom planners and state spaces.
